description:
You are a data-analytics engineer working with a small collection of quarterly sales CSV files.  
Every file lives somewhere underneath  
/home/user/data/csvs/  

and every regular file that ends in “.csv” (case-sensitive) shares an identical first line (column header):

OrderID,Product,Quantity  

What you must deliver
1. Create the directory  
   /home/user/analysis  
   if it does not already exist (do **not** use privileged commands).

2. Produce **one** single‐line terminal command that is built around the classic GNU utilities  
   find … | xargs …  
   (you may add pipes, options, and in-line shell snippets as long as everything is still one compound command) to scan the full tree that starts at /home/user/data/csvs, count the number of data rows (i.e. all lines **after** the header) in every “.csv” file it finds, and write a concise summary file:

   /home/user/analysis/row_counts.log

3. The log file MUST follow this strict format so that automated tests can verify it:

   • One line per CSV file.  
   • Each line contains exactly two comma-separated fields without extra spaces:  
     FILENAME,ROWCOUNT  
     where FILENAME is the basename (no path) of the CSV file and ROWCOUNT is the integer count of data rows.  
   • Lines must be sorted in ordinary ASCII lexicographical order by FILENAME.  
   • The file must end with a single newline (no blank lines before or after the list).

   Example of the required shape (values will differ):

   alpha.csv,12  
   beta.csv,7  
   gamma.csv,3⏎

4. Solve everything with ONE chained command that starts with find and uses xargs somewhere in the pipeline.

Hints you may find useful (but are not prescriptive):
• find supports -print0 and xargs supports -0 for safe filename handling.  
• tail -n +2 can skip a header, wc -l can count lines, and sort can order the final output.  
• Redirect (>>) or (>) only once at the point where you finally create /home/user/analysis/row_counts.log.  

When you are done, the only artefact that will be inspected is /home/user/analysis/row_counts.log; nothing else needs to be printed in your terminal output.
truth:
Initial file-system state supplied to the container
--------------------------------------------------
Directory tree:
/home/user/data/csvs/
├── sales_central.csv
├── sales_east.csv
├── sales_west.csv
└── archive
    └── sales_north.csv

Exact contents of each CSV (note the single final newline in every file):

/home/user/data/csvs/sales_central.csv
OrderID,Product,Quantity
1001,Widget,4
1002,Gadget,2
1003,Doodad,1

/home/user/data/csvs/sales_east.csv
OrderID,Product,Quantity
2001,Thingamabob,5
2002,Widget,3
2003,Gadget,2
2004,Thingy,1

/home/user/data/csvs/sales_west.csv
OrderID,Product,Quantity
3001,Gadget,2
3002,Widget,1

/home/user/data/csvs/archive/sales_north.csv
OrderID,Product,Quantity
4001,Doodad,3
4002,Thingamajig,4
4003,Gizmo,1
4004,Widget,2
4005,Gadget,3


Expected artefact after successful task execution
-------------------------------------------------
File: /home/user/analysis/row_counts.log  
Permissions: regular file, user-writable (default umask OK).  
Content (with a single trailing newline and no extra blank lines):

sales_central.csv,3
sales_east.csv,4
sales_north.csv,5
sales_west.csv,2

The automated test will:
1. Confirm that /home/user/analysis/row_counts.log exists.
2. Read its entire content as text.
3. Verify it matches exactly the four lines (case, commas, order, and newline) shown above.
